text: The surgeon uses a liver retractor to elevate the left lobe of the liver, visible at the top of the screen. The stomach is pulled from the chest cavity, and the hernia sac is delivered out of the chest.
The surgeon uses cautery to divide the tissue attaching the stomach to the chest, pulling the stomach to the right side of the screen to facilitate its delivery from the chest cavity.

The surgeon closes the defect in the diaphragm, through which the stomach previously traveled, by placing sutures on each side of the hole. Adhesions are visible holding the area in place during the hiatal hernia repair.
The surgeon places four permanent sutures to close the defect in the diaphragm, completing the hiatal hernia repair.
After closing the diaphragmatic hole, the surgeon frees the stomach from surrounding attachments. The short gastric vessels on the outer lining of the stomach are divided using surgical instruments.

The surgeon mobilizes the stomach, selecting an appropriate section to pass behind the esophagus. This section is grasped from the opposite side to prepare for the creation of the Nissen fundoplication.

In the background, mesh is visible from the hiatal hernia repair. The surgeon positions the portion of the stomach that has been passed behind the esophagus into the correct location to form the fundoplication.

The surgeon performs a shoe shine maneuver to confirm the correct portion of the stomach for the fundoplication. Sutures are placed from one part of the stomach to the other, incorporating small bites of the esophageal muscular lining to secure the wrap and prevent slippage. An additional suture is added for reinforcement.
The final view shows the completed Nissen fundoplication in its natural position, with the stomach wrapped around the esophagus.
